name: Stack-Effect Type Systems
summary: Concatenative languages describe each word by what it consumes from and produces onto the stack, using composition of stack effects in place of function-type unification. Knaggs formalizes as algebra over stack-effect signatures; StrongForth proves strong static stack-effect checking is feasible in Forth-like languages; Factor extends with row-polymorphic stack effect variables (..a) so combinators talk about 'the rest of the stack'.

sections.title: Overview
sections.content:
Concatenative languages suggest a different path for type-system design: instead of starting from variable-binding terms with ordinary function types, they describe each word by what it consumes from and produces onto the stack, and use composition of stack effects in place of function-type unification.

Knaggs formalizes this as an algebra over stack-effect signatures; StrongForth is a production-style proof that strong static stack-effect checking is feasible in a Forth-like language (typed compile-time stack model, branch/loop consistency, overloads on stack types); Factor extends the idea with **row-polymorphic stack effect variables** such as `..a` so higher-order combinators can talk about "the rest of the stack" — a direct analogue of row polymorphism over stacks rather than records.

sources.label: Knaggs — Type inference in stack based languages

sections.title: StrongForth — static stack-effect checking in production
sections.content:
**StrongForth** demonstrates that Forth's traditionally-untyped stack discipline can be statically checked without losing Forth's character. The compiler maintains a typed compile-time stack model; branches must produce consistent stack shapes at their join point; loops must preserve stack invariants across iterations; overloaded words can dispatch on stack types.

The key insight is that Forth's stack-effect comments (`( a b -- c )`) — already an established documentation convention — can be promoted to typed annotations the compiler enforces. This puts a static type system on top of Forth's idiomatic style without requiring programmers to abandon the discipline.

sections.title: Factor — row-polymorphic stack effect variables
sections.content:
Factor's stack checker extends the static-stack-effect idea with **row variables over stacks**: `..a` stands for an unknown number of stack elements, letting higher-order combinators be polymorphic in the depth of stack they manipulate. A combinator like `dip` (which executes a quotation underneath the top of stack) has the signature `( ..a x quot: ( ..a -- ..b ) -- ..b x )` — `..a` and `..b` are row variables.

This is the cleanest production example of row polymorphism applied to stacks rather than records. The lesson generalises: row-polymorphism's machinery is not specific to records and variants; it can express any "the rest of an unknown-length sequence" abstraction.

A 2017 pluggable-typing thesis adds the lesson that stack-language checking can be configurable from underflow-only to stronger static consistency.

sources.label: Factor paper on stack effects and row polymorphism
